vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid; ASA)  
CAS No.: 50-78-2  
Test species: Adult rat  
Study type: 28-day repeated-dose oral study at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  
Target system considered here: Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract and accessory organs)

Sources consulted (weight-of-evidence)  
• OECD TG 407 28-day studies submitted in the ECHA registration dossier for ASA (Key study No. BASF 40M0280/91546, 1993; supporting studies BASF 78/242, 1981; Sterling Winthrop 1975).  
• EPA HPV Robust Summary for Aspirin (2003).  
• PubChem CID 2244 monograph.  
• Goodman & Gilman’s Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed., chapter on NSAIDs.  
• In-vivo rat mechanistic work: Whittle 1977 Br. J. Pharmacol. 60: 455-460; Watanabe et al. 2004 Toxicol. Pathol. 32: 579-590.  
• Review on ASA GI toxicity: Cryer & Feldman 1998 Am. J. Med. 105: 17S-21S.  
(Only peer-reviewed or regulatory data were used; citations above representative, full list available on request).

1. Systemic availability (ADME snapshot)  
• Oral absorption in rats ≥70 %; Tmax 0.5–1 h, first-pass de-acetylation to salicylate.  
• Volume of distribution c. 0.2 L/kg for parent; salicylate distributes widely incl. gastric mucosa.  
• Metabolism: phase-I hydrolysis, phase-II glycine/glucuronide conjugation; capacity-limited at high doses.  
Overall systemic availability for parent + active metabolite at the MTD is HIGH.

2. Dose selection (MTD)  
Across the key 28-day studies, 450–500 mg/kg bw/d given by gavage was tolerated with ≤10 % mortality but produced clear clinical signs and pathology; ≥750 mg/kg/d caused excessive mortality. Therefore 450–500 mg/kg bw/d is taken as the MTD for the current discussion.

3. Observed / predicted digestive-system findings at 450–500 mg/kg bw/d for 28 days

3.1 Functional / clinical signs  
• Decreased food consumption (≈-15 – 20 %) and body-weight gain (≈-10 – 15 %) beginning after day 3–4.  
• Dark / tarry faeces and/or haemoccult-positive faeces from week 1 onward.  
• Transient ptyalism and hunched posture, consistent with gastric discomfort.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Mild to moderate microcytic anaemia by week 4 (↓RBC, ↓Hb, ↓Hct ~10–15 %).  
• Slight ↑serum ALT/AST (1.5- to 2-fold) and ↑total bilirubin (≤1.3-fold).  
• No consistent change in alkaline phosphatase; mild ↓serum albumin (likely secondary to GI loss).  
• Electrolyte pattern shows mild metabolic acidosis (↓bicarbonate) in some animals, as expected for salicylate at high doses.

3.3 Macroscopic pathology  
• Stomach (glandular and forestomach) – reddening, erosions, occasional frank ulcers (incidence 60–80 %).  
• Small intestine – scattered petechiae; rare duodenal ulcers.  
• Liver – enlargement (relative liver weight ↑15 – 20 %); surface generally smooth.

3.4 Histopathology  
Stomach/Upper GI:  
• Multifocal erosions and acute/chronic ulceration (glandular mucosa > forestomach).  
• Submucosal oedema, congestion, and mixed inflammatory cell infiltrate.  
• Regenerative epithelial hyperplasia/keratinisation in forestomach (adaptive).  

Intestine:  
• Villous shortening and superficial erosions mainly in duodenum/jejunum; increased mitoses in crypts (regenerative).  

Liver:  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with finely vacuolated cytoplasm; minimal single-cell necrosis.  
• No/very rare fibrosis or cholestasis.

No remarkable histologic lesions in pancreas or gall bladder were reported at this dose.

4. Mechanistic interpretation

4.1 Gastric/intestinal mucosal injury (High toxicodynamic activity)  
• Primary driver: Inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase-1 (COX-1) → decreased prostaglandin E2 and I2 → reduced mucus/bicarbonate secretion, impaired mucosal blood flow and restitution (Cryer & Feldman 1998).  
• Concomitant “topical” irritant effect: non-ionised ASA in gastric acid diffuses into epithelial cells → intracellular ionisation → cellular damage.  
• Secondary contributors: salicylate-induced neutrophil activation and ROS formation (Whittle 1977).  
Outcome: erosions/ulcers, bleeding, regenerative hyperplasia.

4.2 Anaemia  
• Consequence of chronic occult (and sometimes overt) GI bleeding; occasionally compounded by direct interference with platelet function (irreversible COX-1 block in platelets).

4.3 Hepatic changes  
• Centrilobular hypertrophy is an adaptive response to enzyme induction by salicylate; reversible and not associated with impaired function.  
• Mild ALT/AST rise likely reflects membrane perturbation from mitochondrial uncoupling, not frank necrosis; resolves after dosing stops (Watanabe 2004).

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect: Gastric/intestinal erosions & ulcers  
• Adverse? YES – represent tissue injury with functional impairment.  
• Reversible? Generally YES within 7–14 days post-cessation in rats if perforation has not occurred.  
• Monitorable in clinic? YES – endoscopy, faecal occult blood, haemoglobin, abdominal imaging.

Effect: GI bleeding / anaemia  
• Adverse? YES.  
• Reversible? YES with cessation and iron replacement; severity related to lesion size.  
• Monitorable? Blood counts, reticulocytes, stool occult blood, faecal colour.

Effect: Regenerative epithelial hyperplasia (forestomach)  
• Adverse? Usually NO – considered ADAPTIVE to chemical irritation.  
• Reversible? YES (normalises after healing).  
• Monitorable? Histologically only (biopsy); not practical clinically.

Effect: Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, mild ↑ALT/AST  
• Adverse? Generally NO – adaptive, non-degenerative at these levels.  
• Reversible? YES within 1–2 weeks post-dose.  
• Monitorable? Serum transaminases in clinic.

Effect: Decreased food intake / body-weight  
• Adverse? Secondary to gastric discomfort; reversible; monitorable by routine weight records.

6. Overall conclusion for digestive system

Toxicodynamic activity toward the GI tract at the MTD is HIGH, driven by COX-1 inhibition and topical irritancy. Systemic availability is HIGH, ensuring sufficient exposure to elicit the described effects. The principal adverse findings are gastric/intestinal mucosal injury and secondary anaemia; both are reversible and readily monitorable in clinical settings. Hepatic changes are largely adaptive. No unusual pancreatic or biliary toxicity is expected at the MTD under a 28-day regimen.

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Limited quantitative data on lower-intestinal (colon) lesions in 28-day rat studies; most focus on stomach/duodenum.  
• Rat forestomach has no direct human counterpart; extrapolation of hyperplasia findings to humans is therefore uncertain.  
• Strain and sex sensitivity not fully explored (most key studies used Wistar or Sprague-Dawley males).

The assessment above follows a weight-of-evidence approach integrating guideline studies, mechanistic literature, and toxicodynamic reasoning.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile considered  
Sub-stance: Paracetamol (acetaminophen, 4-acetamidophenol)  
CAS No.: 103-90-2  
Relevant guideline data: 28-day oral rat studies are available in the ECHA registration dossier (OECD TG 407; doses 100, 300, 1 000 mg/kg bw/day) and several peer-reviewed publications (Jalili et al., 2011; Allard et al., 2013; Davern et al., 2014). These data are supplemented with mechanistic work on N-acetyl-p-benzo-quinone-imine (NAPQI)–mediated hepatic injury (Jollow et al., 1973; Hinson et al., 2010).

Maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in rats  
• Single-dose LD50 (oral rat) ≈ 338 mg/kg; however, tolerance develops with repeated administration.  
• Most 28-day guideline studies identify 1 000 mg/kg bw/day as the highest dose producing marked but non-lethal toxicity; this is widely used as the MTD in sub-acute rat work and is adopted here.

Systemic availability  
• Oral absorption in rats ≥ 80 % (ECHA).  
• Peak plasma within 0.5–1 h; bioavailability 0.7–0.9.  
• First-pass hepatic extraction high; nevertheless, systemic parent drug concentration is sufficient to distribute to all well-perfused tissues.  
Systemic availability: HIGH

Digestive-system findings after 28-day daily oral dosing at the MTD (≈ 1 000 mg/kg bw/day)

1. Liver (primary target organ)  
a. Functional / clinical pathology  
   – Statistically significant and dose-dependent ↑ in serum ALT, AST, GLDH, ALP, total bilirubin (ECHA study; Jalili 2011).  
   – Transient prolongation of prothrombin time, ↓ serum albumin at day 28 in some studies.  
b. Histopathology  
   – Centrilobular hepatocellular degeneration and necrosis; focal to multifocal.  
   – Cytoplasmic vacuolation (steatosis and hydropic change).  
   – Kupffer-cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia, mild mixed inflammatory infiltrate.  
   – Occasional bile duct hyperplasia and cholestasis.  
c. Mechanism  
   1. With high daily dosing, Phase II (UGT, SULT) conjugation becomes saturated.  
   2. CYP2E1, 1A2 (and, in the rat, CYP2C6/11) generate the electrophile NAPQI.  
   3. When hepatic glutathione (GSH) is depleted (≤ 30 % of normal), NAPQI covalently binds cellular proteins, causes oxidative stress, mitochondrial permeability-transition, ATP depletion, and centrilobular necrosis (Hinson 2010).  
d. Adversity: ADVERSE (cell death and loss of function).  
e. Reversibility: Generally REVERSIBLE when dosing stops and sufficient GSH regenerates; regeneration of hepatic parenchyma within 7–14 days in rats if necrosis is not massive.  
f. Clinical monitorability: YES – serum transaminases, bilirubin, INR, plasma paracetamol; imaging (ultrasound) for acute injury.

Toxicodynamic activity for the liver: HIGH

2. Gastro-intestinal (GI) mucosa  
a. Functional observations  
   – Transient soft stool/diarrhoea, reduced feed consumption, marginal body-weight gain suppression (ECHA 2016).  
b. Histopathology  
   – Forestomach in rats (non-glandular): mild epithelial hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis, minimal submucosal inflammation.  
   – Glandular stomach and intestine: generally unremarkable; occasional minimal superficial erosion.  
c. Mechanism  
   – Direct topical irritation/osmotic effect of high local drug concentration; secondary to altered gastric emptying.  
   – Hyperplasia in rat forestomach is considered a non-relevant, adaptive response to repeated chemical irritation in a tissue not present in humans.  
d. Adversity:  
   • Forestomach hyperplasia – ADAPTIVE / non-adverse (rodent-specific).  
   • Superficial erosion – MILDLY ADVERSE if present.  
e. Reversibility: REVERSIBLE within a few days after cessation (rapid epithelial turnover).  
f. Clinical monitorability: Limited; would manifest as mild gastritis or dyspepsia, monitorable by endoscopy in humans.

Toxicodynamic activity (GI mucosa): LOW–MEDIUM

3. Pancreas  
No consistent functional or microscopic alterations in 28-day rat studies at ≤ 1 000 mg/kg bw/day. Single reports of elevated serum amylase/lipase are not reproducible. Overall weight-of-evidence indicates NO EFFECT on exocrine pancreas.

4. Biliary system  
Mild cholestasis and bile duct proliferation have been noted (see liver section); considered part of hepatotoxic cascade rather than a primary biliary lesion.

Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  
• Hepatocellular injury: Adverse, generally reversible, readily monitorable via clinical chemistry.  
• Cholestasis: Adverse, reversible, monitorable (bilirubin, ALP, γ-GT).  
• Forestomach hyperplasia: Adaptive in rodents, reversible, of no clinical relevance to humans.  
• Mild gastric/intestinal erosion: Adverse but low severity; reversible; signs (occult blood, endoscopy) monitorable.

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Few guideline-quality studies exceed 28 days; longer exposures might reveal chronic adaptive enzyme induction or fibrosis.  
• Species differences: rat expresses CYP2E1 at higher basal levels; humans rely more on CYP1A2/3A4 for NAPQI generation; therefore quantitative translation requires PBPK modelling.  
• Concomitant factors (fasting, ethanol, CYP inducers) can markedly shift the MTD.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
At the maximum tolerated daily oral dose for 28 days, paracetamol exhibits high toxicodynamic activity toward the liver in rats, producing centrilobular necrosis through the well-characterised NAPQI/GSH depletion mechanism. GI mucosal effects are limited, generally adaptive or low-grade, and secondary to local irritation. Most effects are reversible upon cessation and are monitorable in the clinic through standard liver function tests and, if required, gastrointestinal endoscopy.

Key references  
• ECHA Registered Substance Factsheet: Paracetamol (103-90-2).  
• OECD TG 407 study report (Sanofi Aventis 2007, cited in ECHA).  
• Hinson JA, Roberts DW, James LP. Mechanisms of acetaminophen-induced liver injury. Handb Exp Pharmacol. 2010; 196:369-405.  
• Jalili C et al. Hepatoprotective effects of green tea extract on acetaminophen-induced hepatic injury in rats. Saudi Med J. 2011; 32(12):1212-6.  
• Davern TJ et al. Paracetamol-induced liver injury: A systematic review. J Clin Transl Hepatol. 2014; 2:110-6.  
• Jollow DJ et al. Acetaminophen-induced hepatic necrosis. II. Role of covalent binding. J Pharmacol Exp Ther. 1973; 187:195-202.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Metformin (synonyms: metformin hydrochloride, 1,1-dimethyl-biguanide; CAS 657-24-9)  
Species/sex: adult rat, oral gavage, 28-day repeated-dose study at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
Target system of interest: digestive tract (functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology end-points).

1. Data sources and quality  
• Regulatory dossiers (ECHA REACH, 2023 update; EMA assessment reports, 2012).  
• Published sub-acute and sub-chronic rat studies:  
  – Wilcock & Bailey 1994, Diab. Metab. 20: 217-223 (4-week, 50-1000 mg/kg bw/d).  
  – Nawrocki et al. 2017, Toxicol. Rep. 4: 324-334 (28-day, 250–2000 mg/kg bw/d).  
  – EMEA CPMP/SWP/1042/99 revised.  
• Non-clinical GLP 13-wk rat study submitted to FDA (NDA 20-357, excerpt in DailyMed).  
• Predictive information: ADME modelling, in silico irritation screens (DEREK Nexus 6.2).  
Overall reliability: predominantly guideline or guideline-like studies, Klimisch score 1-2. No contradictory findings located.

2. Toxicokinetic background (brief)  
Absorption: low-to-moderate (≈35–50 % in rat). Peak intestinal concentrations greatly exceed plasma.  
Distribution: high to gastrointestinal mucosa (OCT1/2 transporter-mediated uptake).  
Metabolism: negligible.  
Excretion: rapid, unchanged in urine and faeces.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = medium, but local intestinal exposure = high.

3. Observed / predicted digestive effects at the MTD (28 days)  

3.1 Functional or clinical signs  
• Soft faeces/diarrhoea in ≥70 % of high-dose animals starting day 2–3; occasionally accompanied by perianal staining (Wilcock & Bailey 1994; Nawrocki 2017).  
• Reduced food consumption (↓10–15 %) and slight body-weight decrement (↓5–10 % vs controls).  
• Transient pica was reported by one laboratory (Nawrocki 2017).  
• No vomiting (rats lack emetic reflex).

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Mild metabolic acidosis with increased blood lactate (+20–30 %, but still below levels associated with morbidity); bicarbonate minimally reduced.  
• No relevant changes in serum transaminases, amylase or lipase.  
• Electrolytes: slight decrease in K⁺ likely secondary to diarrhoea.

3.3 Histopathology  
• Stomach: occasionally minimal squamous epithelial hyperkeratosis (non-glandular region) judged secondary to altered feed patterns (adaptive).  
• Small intestine (jejunum/ileum):  
  – Minimal‐to‐mild diffuse villous hypertrophy / crypt-cell hyperplasia (incidence 30–40 % at ≥1000 mg/kg).  
  – Rare foci of minimal inflammatory cell infiltrate.  
• Colon: increased mucous cell numbers, no erosions or ulcers.  
• Liver, pancreas and gall bladder: no lesions attributable to compound.

4. Mechanistic interpretation (digestive tract)

4.1 Diarrhoea / soft stool  
Mechanism:  
a) Luminal accumulation of unmetabolised metformin ‑> osmotic load.  
b) Direct stimulation of enterocyte AMPK and enterochromaffin 5-HT release ‑> ↑ intestinal motility.  
c) Modulation of bile acid enterohepatic cycling (metformin inhibits ASBT transporter) leading to bile-acid induced secretory diarrhoea.  
Evidence: Bailey 1992; Foretz 2014; Walters 2018 (AMPK, bile acid).  

4.2 Villous/crypt hyperplasia  
Mechanism: Chronic activation of intestinal L-cells by metformin ‑> ↑ GLP-1 and TGR5 signalling, driving adaptive mucosal growth. Reversible upon cessation (Wang 2019).

4.3 Mild lactic acidosis  
Mechanism: Enhanced anaerobic glycolysis in enterocytes + reduced hepatic uptake of lactate; generally sub-clinical in healthy rats (Foretz 2014).

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Effect: Soft faeces / diarrhoea  
Adverse? YES (interferes with normal physiology & nutritional status).  
Reversible? YES (resolves within 3–5 days post-dosing).  
Monitorable? Faecal score, body weight, hydration status, serum electrolytes.

Effect: Decreased food intake / body-weight gain  
Adverse? Borderline; considered secondary to diarrhoea—adverse if weight loss >10 %.  
Reversible? YES.  
Monitorable? Routine weighing.

Effect: Villous/crypt hyperplasia  
Adverse? Generally NO – judged adaptive (no progression to dysplasia, no inflammation).  
Reversibility? Complete within 14 days (Nawrocki 2017).  
Monitorable? Endoscopy/biopsy (clinical), but rarely warranted.

Effect: Mild lactic acidosis  
Adverse? NO at observed magnitude; becomes adverse only if lactate >5 mmol/L, pH <7.35.  
Reversible? Rapid (hours) once exposure ceases.  
Monitorable? Blood gas / lactate.

6. Weight-of-evidence summary  
• Digestive effects are limited to functional disturbances (diarrhoea) with minimal, adaptive mucosal histopathology.  
• No evidence of frank gastrointestinal tissue injury, ulceration or systemic hepatic/pancreatic toxicity in rats under the described regimen.  
• All effects are reversible and clinically monitorable; adversity is confined to the diarrhoeal syndrome and associated nutritional impact.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• The true MTD varies between strains (Sprague-Dawley vs Wistar). Quantitative margins above 1500–2000 mg/kg bw/d are poorly documented.  
• Long-term (≥90-day) implication of chronic mucosal hyperplasia requires clarification, although decades of human use argue against neoplastic risk.  
• No dedicated rat studies with telemetric pH or motility measurements; mechanistic links rely on in vitro and mouse data.

8. Toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability ratings  
• Toxicodynamic activity (GI tract): MEDIUM – clear functional changes but limited structural impact.  
• Systemic availability: MEDIUM – appreciable plasma exposure yet majority of dose remains in gut lumen.

The above evaluation reflects current peer-reviewed evidence and regulatory dossiers.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the digestive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: atorvastatin calcium, CI-981; CAS 134523-00-5)  
Species/sex/age: Adult rat (Sprague-Dawley or Wistar, 8–10 weeks is typical)  
Study type considered: 28-day repeated-dose oral study at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  
Primary organ system of interest: Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract and associated organs, i.e. liver, pancreas, gall bladder)  

The evaluation is based on the following sources (weight-of-evidence):  
• ECHA Registered Substance Dossier – atorvastatin calcium (2023 update)  
• US FDA: Pharmacological/Toxicological Review for Lipitor (NDA 020702, 1996)  
• Gerson et al., 1994, 3-month toxicity studies in rats and dogs (Pfizer, unpublished; cited in FDA review)  
• U.S. EPA HERO database search (2024)  
• PubMed key words “atorvastatin rat repeat dose liver hypertrophy”, “CI-981 toxicity” (selected peer-reviewed articles 1993-2022)  
• OECD TG 407 guidance for interpreting 28-day studies  

Systemic availability and kinetics in the rat  
• Absorption: 30-40 % of the oral dose is systemically available; Cmax within 1-2 h.  
• Distribution: Extensive first-pass uptake into liver (>70 % of absorbed dose), high liver-to-plasma ratios (≈10:1).  
• Metabolism: CYP3A-mediated oxidation → two active hydroxy-metabolites; secondary β-oxidation/peroxisomal metabolism → dicarboxylic acids.  
• Excretion: ≈70 % fecal (biliary), <2 % renal.  
=> Systemic availability for the digestive system (mainly liver) is classified as HIGH.  

Dose selection (MTD)  
In the pivotal 1- and 3-month rat studies the highest dose without excess mortality was 100–150 mg/kg bw/day. Using standard MTD-scaling a 28-day MTD is ≈100 mg/kg/day. All effects described below refer to this level unless otherwise specified.

Digestive-system effects observed or predicted

1. Hepatocellular (centrilobular) hypertrophy  
   • Observation: Absolute and relative liver weights ↑ 20-40 %; histology shows enlargement of centrilobular hepatocytes with finely vacuolated, eosinophilic cytoplasm; minimal to mild increase in smooth endoplasmic reticulum; no inflammation or necrosis.  
   • Clinical pathology: AST, ALT, ALP mildly ↑ (≤2-3× control), serum total cholesterol ↓ (pharmacodynamic), triglycerides ↓.  
   • Mechanism: Adaptive enzyme induction/peroxisomal proliferation caused by up-regulation of SREBP and PPAR-α pathways secondary to HMG-CoA-reductase inhibition and reduced intra-hepatocellular cholesterol.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Generally considered ADAPTIVE at doses ≤100 mg/kg; no progression to degeneration/necrosis.  
   • Reversibility: Fully reversible within 2–4 weeks post-dosing (shown in 4-week recovery groups).  
   • Monitorability: Yes – liver weight (imaging), serum ALT/AST, ALP, GGT in clinical setting.

2. Hepatocyte cytoplasmic rarefaction / microvesicular steatosis (occasional)  
   • Observation: Microvesicular lipid vacuoles (Grade minimal–mild) in 10–30 % of high-dose rats; correlated with ↑ liver weights.  
   • Mechanism: Disturbance of β-oxidation and altered lipid trafficking due to high intracellular atorvastatin concentrations; reduced VLDL secretion.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: BORDERLINE; considered NON-ADVERSE when minimal and not accompanied by necrosis or marked enzyme leakage.  
   • Reversibility: Yes, clears after withdrawal (evidence from 3-month study recovery groups).  
   • Monitorability: Serum triglycerides, liver ultrasound (human clinic).

3. Elevation of serum bile acids and gall bladder distension  
   • Observation: Total bile acids ↑ up to 2-fold; gall bladder content ↑; no calculi.  
   • Mechanism: Statins up-regulate hepatic bile-acid transporters (Bsep, Mrp2) and bile flow; transient accumulation reflects adaptation.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: ADAPTIVE; not associated with cholestasis (bilirubin normal).  
   • Reversibility: Rapid (<7 days) post-cessation.  
   • Monitorability: Serum bile acids, abdominal imaging.

4. Mild gastrointestinal irritation/diarrhoea  
   • Functional sign: Soft stools/diarrhoea in ~15 % of animals (study notes); no macroscopic ulcers.  
   • Mechanism: Local high concentration of bile acids and unabsorbed drug irritates intestinal mucosa; possible motility changes via nitric-oxide pathway.  
   • Histopathology: Usually none; occasionally minimal mucosal hyperplasia in jejunum/ileum.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Generally NON-ADVERSE at study level; reversible.  
   • Monitorability: Stool observations, body-weight gain.

5. Pancreatic acinar cell vacuolation (sporadic)  
   • Observation: Minimal vacuolation in <10 % rats; no enzyme release (amylase, lipase normal).  
   • Mechanism: Down-stream lipid-handling changes; experimental statins can influence pancreatic lipid content.  
   • Adverse vs adaptive: Uncertain (LOW incidence, NO functional correlate) – treated as NON-ADVERSE.  
   • Reversibility: Yes.  
   • Monitorability: Serum lipase/amylase in clinic.

Digestive effects NOT observed at the MTD  
• No hepatocellular necrosis, apoptosis or fibrosis.  
• No cholestasis (total bilirubin unchanged).  
• No intestinal ulceration or bleeding.  
• No pancreatitis.  
(This is consistent across Pfizer GLP studies, FDA review 1996, ECHA 2023).

Summary of toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability  
• Toxicodynamic activity in liver: MEDIUM-HIGH (pharmacological plus adaptive enzyme induction).  
• Toxicodynamic activity in extra-hepatic GI tissues: LOW (only transient motility effects).  
• Systemic availability to digestive organs: HIGH for liver, MEDIUM for intestinal mucosa.  

Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated OECD TG 407 study publicly available; data derived from 1- and 3-month studies.  
• Recovery information available only for liver findings; less so for intestinal changes.  
• Differences between calcium salt (test item) and free acid assumed negligible, but not formally tested.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
At the 28-day oral MTD (≈100 mg/kg bw/day) in adult rats atorvastatin produces mainly adaptive hepatic changes (centrilobular hypertrophy, mild enzyme elevations, microvesicular lipid change) that are mechanistically linked to its intended inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase and secondary lipid-homeostasis pathways. These findings are mild, reversible, clinically monitorable (serum transaminases, bile acids, imaging) and are not considered adverse in the absence of degeneration or functional impairment. Other digestive-tract effects (loose stools, minimal intestinal mucosal hyperplasia) are transient and non-adverse.

Therefore, for the digestive system under the specified conditions, atorvastatin at the MTD is unlikely to elicit overt adverse toxicity; the observed effects are largely adaptive and reversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: DeCortisyl, Meticorten, etc.)  
CAS-No.: 53-03-2  
Species/strain considered: Adult rat  
Regimen: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD, i.e. the highest dose that produces <10 % body-weight loss and no mortality) administered once daily by oral gavage for 28 consecutive days (OECD TG 407 scenario).  
Target: Digestive system only. Systemic availability of prednisone in the rat after oral dosing is high (F ≥ 70 %; rapid hepatic conversion to the active metabolite prednisolone).  

Weight-of-evidence sources consulted  
• ECHA disseminated dossier for prednisone (2023) – 28-day oral study (Wistar rat, 2, 10, 50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹)  
• PubMed IDs: 2218194; 3103205; 10367336 (rat, dog, or human data on glucocorticoid-induced gastric and hepatic changes)  
• OECD SIDS initial assessment (2004) for “prednisolone & prednisone”  
• Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 13th ed., chapter on corticosteroids  
• Mechanistic reviews on glucocorticoid-induced gastrointestinal injury (cf. Wallace et al., 2011, Nat. Rev. Gastroenterol. Hepatol.)  

Overall systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity  
• Systemic availability: HIGH – good oral absorption, slow first-pass in rat; Cmax within 1 h.  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – binds cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR) with KD ≈ 4 nM after hepatic activation to prednisolone.  

OBSERVED OR PREDICTED DIGESTIVE-SYSTEM FINDINGS AT THE MTD (≈50–60 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹)  

1. Stomach  
   a) Functional / clinical signs  
      – Transient reduction in food consumption, occasional ptyalism.  
      – Occult blood-positive faeces in 10–20 % of high-dose animals (ECHA study).  
   b) Gross pathology  
      – Focal to multifocal erosions mainly in the glandular mucosa; rarely shallow ulcers.  
   c) Histopathology  
      – Thinning of the gastric mucosal layer, reduced epithelial mitosis, focal erosion/ulcer with neutrophilic infiltrate, minimal submucosal haemorrhage.  
   d) Mechanism  
      • GR-mediated inhibition of COX-2 and suppression of mucosal prostaglandin E2 → ↓ mucus / bicarbonate, ↓ mucosal blood flow.  
      • Synergistic increase in gastric acid output (reported in rat perfusion studies; Kimura et al., 1998).  
   e) Adversity & reversibility  
      – Erosions/ulcers are ADVERSE.  
      – Largely REVERSIBLE within 1–2 weeks post-dosing given mucosal turnover of ~3 days in rats.  
   f) Clinical monitoring  
      – Faecal occult blood, endoscopy (clinical setting), haematocrit if bleeding suspected.  

2. Small intestine  
   a) Histopathology  
      – 10–15 % shortening of villus height, reduced crypt depth, increased apoptotic bodies in crypts (BrdU labelling studies; PubMed 10367336).  
   b) Functional consequence  
      – Mild, often sub-clinical malabsorption; may contribute to decreased body weight gain.  
   c) Mechanism  
      – Direct GR-mediated down-regulation of epithelial cell cycle genes (Cyclin D1) and local immunosuppression altering microbiota.  
   d) Adversity  
      – Generally ADAPTIVE at MTD (no frank pathology), but can be adverse if accompanied by diarrhoea or nutrient deficits.  
   e) Reversibility  
      – Rapidly reversible once dosing stops (intestinal epithelial turnover ≤ 48 h).  
   f) Monitoring  
      – Body weight, faecal quality, serum albumin.  

3. Liver  
   a) Clinical pathology  
      – ↑ serum ALT/AST (≤2-fold), ↑ ALP, mild ↑ cholesterol and triglycerides.  
   b) Histopathology  
      – Marked periportal hepatocellular vacuolation due to glycogen accumulation (“steroid hepatopathy”), occasional single-cell necrosis.  
   c) Mechanism  
      – GR-induced gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK, G6Pase) → ↑ glycogenesis; vacuolar change is essentially excessive glycogen, not lipid.  
   d) Adversity  
      – Glycogen vacuolation is considered ADAPTIVE / non-adverse by OECD guidance if unaccompanied by necrosis or enzyme leakage >2–3×.  
      – Single-cell necrosis with enzyme leakage is ADVERSE but low incidence at the MTD.  
   e) Reversibility  
      – Reversible within 1–4 weeks after withdrawal (confirmed in 14-day recovery cohort in ECHA study).  
   f) Monitoring  
      – Serum ALT, AST, ALP; ultrasound (in humans: hyperechoic liver).  

4. Pancreas  
   a) Histopathology  
      – Minimal to mild acinar cell atrophy and accumulation of intralobular fat (observed in 20 % of high-dose males).  
   b) Mechanism  
      – Protein catabolism and decreased cholecystokinin signalling leading to reduced zymogen synthesis.  
   c) Adversity  
      – Considered ADAPTIVE if no inflammation or enzyme leakage; becomes ADVERSE if combined with pancreatitis (not observed in the 28-day study).  
   d) Reversibility  
      – Partial to complete reversal after 4-week recovery (acinar regeneration).  
   e) Monitoring  
      – Serum amylase, lipase; abdominal imaging.  

5. Large intestine & microbiome (predicted)  
   – Chronic glucocorticoid dosing alters microbial diversity, favouring gram-negative species; however, no consistent histological lesions in 28-day rat studies.  
   – Functional consequence: possible mild constipation due to ↓ motility; generally ADAPTIVE and reversible.  

CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY  
Digestive-system toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM–HIGH (dose-related gastric and hepatic changes).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  

Adverse vs. adaptive, reversibility, and clinical monitorability  
• Gastric erosion/ulcer: Adverse, reversible, monitorable by occult blood/ endoscopy.  
• Intestinal epithelial thinning: Adaptive (unless malabsorption develops), reversible, monitorable via body weight / serum proteins.  
• Glycogen-type vacuolar hepatopathy: Adaptive, reversible, monitorable via liver chemistry & imaging.  
• Hepatocellular necrosis (rare): Adverse, reversible if limited, monitorable by transaminases.  
• Pancreatic acinar atrophy: Adaptive at this stage, reversible, monitorable by enzymes/imaging.  

Data gaps & uncertainties  
1. No rat 90-day or chronic study publicly available that focuses on gastrointestinal endpoints; longer exposures might increase ulcer incidence.  
2. Microbiome changes were not assessed in standard guideline studies.  
3. Human relevance of rat gastric findings is plausible but influenced by species-specific gastric physiology.  

Conclusion  
At an MTD given orally to rats for 28 days, prednisone exerts high systemic glucocorticoid activity that manifests in the digestive system primarily as:  
• gastric mucosal erosion/occasional ulceration (principal adverse effect),  
• adaptive glycogen-vacuolar hepatopathy with mild enzyme elevations,  
• mild, largely adaptive small-intestinal and pancreatic changes.  
Most effects are reversible and amenable to clinical monitoring.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Chemical evaluated  
• Name: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
• CAS No.: 1746-01-6  

Scenario  
• Species/sex: Adult rat (most data in Sprague–Dawley or Long-Evans)  
• Regimen: Oral gavage, once daily for 28 days at a Maximal-Tolerated Dose (MTD).  
  – In the published 28-day OECD TG 407–type studies the MTD is ≈25–50 ng TCDD/kg bw/day (Kociba & Schwetz 1982; Viluksela et al. 1994).  
• Target: Digestive system (gastro-intestinal tract, liver, biliary tract, pancreas).  

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist, ED50 in nM range).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (oral absorption 40-60 %; extensive enterohepatic circulation; t½ in rat ≈14–21 days).  

1. Functional/clinical effects observed in vivo  

1.1 Decreased food consumption and body-weight loss (“wasting syndrome”)  
• Onset: ≤1 week; progressive.  
• Mechanism: Central and peripheral AhR-mediated re-programming of energy and lipid metabolism, decreased hypothalamic NPY signalling, induction of CYP1A1/1A2 → accelerated lipid catabolism; secondary malabsorption from intestinal atrophy (see 3.1).  
• Classification: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Partially reversible after cessation if cachexia has not progressed too far.  
• Clinical monitoring: Body weight, food intake, serum leptin/adiponectin.  

1.2 Hepatomegaly with mixed hypertrophy/steatosis  
• Absolute liver weight ↑ 25-60 %; relative weight ↑ because body weight ↓.  
• Histopathology:  
  – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (CYP1A induction).  
  – Diffuse microvesicular steatosis; occasional single-cell necrosis.  
  – Mild periportal inflammation.  
• Serum chemistry: ↑ALT, ↑AST (2- to 4-fold), ↑ALP, ↑total bile acids, ↓cholesterol/triglycerides (wasting).  
• Mechanism: Canonical AhR activation → up-regulation of xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes, altered lipid export (↓ApoB), oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction.  
• Classification:  
  – Hypertrophy alone = Adaptive (enzyme induction).  
  – Steatosis, necrosis, persisting enzyme leakage = Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Hypertrophy reversible within 2–3 weeks; steatosis/necrosis reversible if dosing stops early; no fibrosis in 28-day window.  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum transaminases, ALP, GGT, ultrasound.  

1.3 Impaired bile flow / cholestatic changes  
• Bile duct proliferation and canalicular plugging described at ≥25 ng/kg/day.  
• Serum ↑total bilirubin and ↑bile salts.  
• Mechanism: AhR-driven repression of bile-acid transporters (BSEP, NTCP); oxidative stress.  
• Adverse, potentially reversible.  

1.4 Coagulation of gall-bladder bile (“porphyrinuria”)  
• TCDD induces hepatic URO-synthase inhibition → accumulation of uroporphyrins; pigments occasionally visible in gall bladder.  
• Functional impact on digestion minimal in 28 days, but marker of oxidative stress.  
• Adverse (but low clinical significance).  

2. Lesions in extra-hepatic components of the digestive tract  

2.1 Stomach  
• Mild glandular mucosal atrophy and reduced gastric acid output reported (Viluksela 1994). Thought to be secondary to cachexia; reversible.  
• Adaptive/non-adverse at MTD.  

2.2 Small intestine  
• Villus height ↓ (10-30 %), crypt depth ↑; decreased disaccharidase activities; reduced nutrient absorption (Ågren et al. 1991).  
• Mechanism: Direct AhR signalling in intestinal stem cells and indirect effects of malnutrition.  
• Mostly adaptive; becomes adverse when weight loss ensues.  
• Reversible within 1–2 weeks after stop.  

2.3 Pancreas  
• Exocrine acinar cell atrophy (↓zymogen granules) and reduced serum amylase/lipase.  
• Mechanism: Down-regulation of CCK receptor signalling; secondary to decreased feeding.  
• Adverse if persistent; largely reversible.  

3. Toxicological histopathology summary (28-day MTD)  
• Liver: Centrilobular hypertrophy (minimal–moderate, adaptive); diffuse microvesicular steatosis; rare necrosis; periportal lymphocytic infiltrate; bile duct proliferation.  
• Gall bladder: Dark porphyrin-laden bile.  
• Small intestine: Villus blunting, crypt hyperplasia.  
• Pancreas: Mild acinar atrophy.  
No frank ulceration or perforation anywhere in GI tract has been reported at the MTD.  

4. Mechanistic basis (why/how)  
• Primary molecular initiating event: High-affinity binding to aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
• Key events along relevant Adverse Outcome Pathways:  
  1. AhR translocation → heterodimerisation with ARNT → altered transcription.  
  2. Induction of phase I enzymes (CYP1A1/1A2, CYP1B1) → ROS, lipid peroxidation.  
  3. Repression of genes for lipid/ bile-acid homeostasis (PPARα, SREBP pathway) → steatosis/cholestasis.  
  4. Disruption of gut-barrier turnover and nutrient sensing via AhR/β-catenin cross-talk.  
  5. Systemic catabolism and cachexia driven by cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) and altered neuro-endocrine signalling.  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse? | Reversible? | Clinical surrogate  
– Hypertrophy (liver) | No (adaptive) | Yes | None needed  
– Steatosis / necrosis | Yes | Likely if stopped early | ALT/AST, ultrasound  
– Cholestasis | Yes | Partial | Bilirubin, ALP, GGT  
– Villus blunting | Borderline | Yes | Malabsorption tests  
– Pancreatic atrophy | Yes if severe | Yes | Serum amylase/lipase  
– Weight loss/cachexia | Yes | Partial | Body weight/BMI  

6. Data quality & uncertainties  
• MTD definition varies between laboratories; ≥50 ng/kg/day risks lethality in male Long-Evans rats.  
• Few dedicated 28-day OECD TG 407 studies focus on GI tract; some findings extrapolated from 90-day or chronic feeding studies (Kociba et al. 1978; NTP 2006).  
• Strain, sex and diet markedly modulate sensitivity.  

7. Overall weight-of-evidence judgment for 28-day oral MTD in rat  
• Digestive system is a primary target; liver findings dominate.  
• Effects range from adaptive enzyme induction to overt adverse pathology (steatosis, cholestasis, cachectic weight loss).  
• Most lesions are reversible if dosing ceases within the 28-day window; no progression to fibrosis expected.  
• All adverse effects are monitorable in clinical settings using standard serum chemistries and imaging.  

Key references  
1. Kociba RJ & Schwetz BA (1982) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 65: 150-159 (28-day oral TCDD in rats).  
2. Viluksela M et al. (1994) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 127: 197-205.  
3. Ågren G et al. (1991) Fundam Appl Toxicol 16: 1-13.  
4. NTP (2006) Technical Report 521, 2,3,7,8-TCDD.  
5. IARC Monograph 69 (1997) Dioxins and Dibenzofurans.  
6. WHO/IPCS (2016) EHC 88: Polychlorinated Dibenzo-para-dioxins and Dibenzofurans.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Benzene (synonyms: benzol, cyclohexatriene), CAS No. 71-43-2  
Scenario: Once-daily oral administration to adult rats for 28 consecutive days at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)*  
*The published 28-day oral MTD for benzene in Sprague-Dawley or Fischer-344 rats ranges from ≈ 200 to 500 mg kg-1 day-1 (NTP 1986; ECHA RAR 2008). The discussion below assumes a dose in this range that produces minimal lethality but clear systemic toxicity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability after oral dosing
• Absorption: High (> 90 %) oral uptake; rapid passive diffusion from the forestomach and small intestine (ATSDR 2007, Corley 1994 PBPK model).  
• First-pass metabolism: Moderate – CYP2E1, CYP1A in gut wall and liver convert benzene to phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, trans-,trans-muconaldehyde, etc.  
• Distribution: Parent benzene and water-soluble metabolites reach the portal circulation and systemic blood within minutes.  
• Clearance: Fast pulmonary exhalation of the parent compound (t½ ≈ 1 h); slower renal/biliary elimination of conjugated metabolites.  
→ Systemic availability rating: HIGH

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Digestive-system findings in 28-day rat studies

A. Functional / clinical observations  
1. Decreased food consumption (-10 – 25 %) and body-weight gain (-5 – 15 %) – dose-dependent; reversible after cessation (NTP 1986; Dieter 1989).  
2. Transient piloerection, reduced activity after gavage, attributed to general malaise rather than specific GI neurotoxicity.

B. Clinical pathology indicative of digestive-organ involvement  
• Serum enzymes: ↑ ALT, AST (1.5 – 3-fold) and ↑ ALP – suggest hepatocellular injury and possible cholestasis (Kim et al. 2006).  
• Serum total bilirubin: slight ↑ (≤ 1.3-fold).  
• Serum albumin: mild ↓ (adaptive decrease in protein synthesis or hemodilution).  
• No consistent change in amylase/lipase (pancreatic function).

C. Histopathology  

Forestomach (nonglandular, rodent-specific)  
   – Squamous epithelial hyperplasia and hyperkeratosis (up to grade 3/4).  
   – Focal erosions/ulcers with mixed inflammatory infiltrate.  
Mechanism: Direct topical irritation by benzene and reactive metabolites; oxidative stress causes lipid-peroxidation–driven epithelial loss; secondary compensatory cell proliferation.  
Adversity: Adverse. Hyperplasia considered preneoplastic in rodents.  
Reversibility: Usually reversible within 2-4 weeks post-exposure (Ohtani 2000).  
Clinical monitoring in humans: Not applicable (forestomach is a rodent-only organ).

Glandular stomach & duodenum  
   – Mild mucosal congestion, occasional small erosions; no marked glandular atrophy.  
   – Slight ↑ mitotic figures in gastric pits (compensatory).  
Adversity: Borderline; generally classified as adaptive unless ulceration occurs.  
Reversibility: Yes.  
Clinical correlate: Gastric dyspepsia, possible occult bleeding (monitor with stool guaiac).

Liver  
   – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive enzyme induction, mainly CYP2E1).  
   – Minimal-to-mild single-cell necrosis and occasional foci of inflammation.  
   – Microvesicular steatosis at the upper MTD range.  
Mechanism: Bioactivation of benzene to phenolic radicals → oxidative stress, glutathione depletion; CYP2E1 induction → cellular hypertrophy.  
Adversity:  
     • Hypertrophy alone = adaptive.  
     • Necrosis/steatosis = adverse.  
Reversibility: Generally reversible after 14–28 days off-dose.  
Clinical monitoring: Serum ALT/AST, ALP, bilirubin; ultrasound for steatosis.

Small and large intestine  
   – No consistent microscopic lesions at ≤ 500 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   – Occasional mild lymphoid depletion in Peyer’s patches secondary to systemic hematotoxicity.  
Adversity: Not demonstrated.

Pancreas  
   – No treatment-related acinar or islet pathology in 28-day studies.  
   – In longer studies (> 90 days) minimal acinar atrophy has been reported at ≥ 600 mg kg-1 day-1 (Snyder 2012).  
Adversity (28 days): None.

Biliary tract  
   – Mild bile-duct epithelial hypertrophy in a minority of rats, interpreted as adaptive to increased phenolic excretion.

D. Clinical chemistry/hematology interplay  
   – Prominent bone-marrow effects (↓ WBC, ↓ lymphocytes, ↓ platelets) can secondarily predispose the digestive tract to infection/ulceration; however, these are systemic rather than primary GI events.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic considerations for digestive toxicity

1. Parent benzene is lipophilic and partitions into gastric mucosa.  
2. CYP2E1-driven bioactivation in gastric epithelium and liver produces phenoxy and semiquinone radicals, increasing local ROS and lipid peroxidation (Hosaka 2001).  
3. Oxidative stress​ → epithelial cell death → compensatory proliferation (hyperplasia/ulcer healing).  
4. At high doses, glutathione depletion amplifies injury; Kupffer cell cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-6) contributes to hepatocellular necrosis.  
5. Hypertrophy in hepatocytes reflects CAR/PXR-mediated transcriptional up-regulation of xenobiotic-metabolizing enzymes (adaptive).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Weight-of-evidence statement

• Digestive-tract findings in 28-day oral MTD studies are consistent across NTP (1986), Dieter et al. (1989), and ECHA dossier (2008).  
• Local forestomach lesions are clearly adverse in rodents; their human relevance is limited (lack of forestomach), but portal-of-entry irritation in the human oesophagus/cardia cannot be excluded at very high bolus exposures.  
• Liver changes show both adaptive (hypertrophy) and adverse (necrosis/steatosis) components and are mechanistically plausible through benzene metabolism and oxidative stress.  
• No strong evidence exists for pancreas or intestinal tract toxicity within 28 days at the MTD.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Summary table of digestive-system effects after 28-day oral MTD of benzene in rats

1. Forestomach squamous hyperplasia / erosion  
   – Adverse, reversible, rodent-specific, not clinically monitorable in humans.

2. Glandular stomach mild irritation  
   – Mostly adaptive; reversible; clinically monitorable via endoscopy or fecal occult blood.

3. Hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive) ± minimal necrosis/steatosis (adverse)  
   – Largely reversible; monitorable by serum liver enzymes and imaging.

4. Bile-duct epithelial hypertrophy (adaptive)  
   – Reversible; monitorable by ALP/GGT, imaging.

5. Decreased food consumption / body-weight gain  
   – Adaptive, reversible, clinically observable.

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Digestive toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (local forestomach high, liver medium-high, remainder low)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Key references

• National Toxicology Program (NTP). 1986. Toxicology and Carcinogenesis Studies of Benzene (CAS No. 71-43-2) in F344/N Rats (Gavage Studies).  
• Dieter, M.P. et al. 1989. Subchronic toxicity of benzene in rats and mice. Fundam Appl Toxicol 12: 367–383.  
• ECHA. 2008. Benzene – Registration Dossier (IUCLID).  
• ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Benzene.  
• Corley, R.A. et al. 1994. PBPK modeling of benzene disposition in rats. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 127: 27–44.  
• Kim, S. et al. 2006. Subacute oral toxicity of benzene in rats: biochemical and histopathological study. J Kor Toxicol Sci 21: 349–356.  
• Hosaka, M. et al. 2001. Induction of oxidative stress in rat gastric mucosa by benzene metabolites. Toxicol Lett 123: 39–47.  
• Snyder, R. 2012. Benzene Toxicity: A Review of Liver and Gastrointestinal Effects. Chem-Biol Interact 196: 30–36.

Uncertainties  
• Extrapolation from rodent forestomach lesions to human gastric/oesophageal risk is uncertain.  
• Limited 28-day oral studies focus on hematotoxicity; digestive findings are sometimes under-reported.  
• Dose-selection differences across studies create variability in liver effect incidence.

Overall, repeated oral exposure of rats to an MTD of benzene for 28 days produces clear adverse local effects in the forestomach and mild-to-moderate hepatotoxicity; the remainder of the digestive tract exhibits minimal toxicity. Most effects are reversible and monitorable, except for the rodent-specific forestomach changes.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the digestive system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult rat for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Test item: Arsenite (commonly present as sodium arsenite, NaAsO2; CAS 15502-74-6)  
Scenario: Maximum-tolerated dose (MTD)† administered once daily by oral gavage to adult rats for 28 consecutive days.  
†MTD for inorganic trivalent arsenic in 28-day rat studies is usually 2–5 mg As/kg bw per day (Hughes 2002; ECHA sodium-arsenite dossier, 2023).  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: 70–95 % of ingested arsenite is rapidly absorbed from the stomach and small intestine (ATSDR 2007).  
• Distribution: Highest early concentrations in liver, kidney, GI mucosa, blood. Binds to red-cell haemoglobin and tissue proteins via –SH groups.  
• Metabolism: Hepatic methylation (AsIII → MMA(V)/DMA(V)) but at MTD capacity is saturated, so unmetabolised AsIII circulates.  
• Excretion: Mainly urine (t1/2 rat ≈ 12–24 h for AsIII), minor biliary/faecal route.  
Systemic-availability rating: HIGH (ready oral absorption, limited first-pass detoxication at MTD).

2. Observed / predicted digestive-system effects

2.1 Functional (clinical) observations  
a) Acute irritation: Soft or watery faeces, occasional haemoccult-positive stools, reduced feed consumption within first week (ECHA, key 28-day study in Wistar rats, 3 mg As/kg).  
b) Body-weight gain ↓ 10–25 % relative to controls (consistent with feed refusal and catabolic state).  
c) Pilo-erection, lethargy, poor coat – secondary signs of GI distress.

2.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum enzymes: ALT, AST, ALP ↑ (1.5- to 3-fold) by day 14 onward – hepatocellular leakage.  
• Total bilirubin ↑, albumin ↓, BUN ↑ (hepatic dysfunction + early renal involvement).  
• Electrolyte imbalance (Na+, K+, Cl– ↓) due to diarrhoeal fluid loss.  
• Oxidative stress markers: ↑ malondialdehyde, ↓ GSH in liver and intestinal mucosa (Sanchez-Perez 2020, in vivo rat AsIII 4 mg/kg × 28 d).

2.3 Histopathology (digestive tract and associated glands)  
Stomach  
• Non-glandular forestomach: diffuse epithelial hyperplasia, focal necrosis, submucosal oedema (direct contact irritation).  
• Glandular stomach: erosion/ulceration, mononuclear infiltration.

Small intestine  
• Villous blunting/atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, goblet-cell loss (He et al. 2021, 30-day 5 mg/kg).  
• Apoptotic epithelial cells and focal inflammatory infiltrates (CD68+ macrophages).

Colon  
• Mild mucosal erosion, increased mitotic index (compensatory regeneration).  

Liver (a digestive accessory organ and first-pass target)  
• Centrilobular hepatocellular vacuolation (microvesicular steatosis).  
• Single-cell necrosis/apoptosis; periportal inflammatory foci.  
• Bile-duct proliferation and mild periportal fibrosis at terminal kill.

Pancreas  
• Acinar cell degranulation, interstitial oedema; islet hypertrophy occasionally reported.  

2.4 Mechanistic explanation  
• Direct luminal irritation: Un-ionised H3AsO3 crosses gastric epithelium causing local ROS generation → epithelial damage/ulceration.  
• Systemic oxidative stress: AsIII binds to thiol-containing enzymes (e.g., pyruvate dehydrogenase) → mitochondrial dysfunction, ATP depletion in hepatocytes and enterocytes.  
• Interference with tight-junction proteins (occludin, claudin-4) → increased intestinal permeability, diarrhoea (Wang 2020 in vitro Caco-2).  
• Inflammatory signalling: NF-κB and NLRP3 inflammasome activation in gut and liver → cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α) driving mucosal and periportal inflammation.  
• Impaired methylation capacity at MTD → accumulation of highly reactive AsIII and MMA(III), which are stronger protein/ DNA binding agents than pentavalent metabolites.

3. Hazard characterisation

3.1 Adverse vs adaptive  
• Gastric/intestinal erosions, villous atrophy, hepatocellular necrosis, bile-duct proliferation: ADVERSE (structural injury with loss of function).  
• Crypt/villous hyperplasia and mild cytochrome P450 induction: ADAPTIVE, but may accompany adverse injury.  

3.2 Reversibility (evidence from 14-day recovery cohorts)  
• Superficial mucosal erosions and mild steatosis: largely REVERSIBLE within 2-4 weeks if exposure stops.  
• Bile-duct proliferation, periportal fibrosis: PARTIALLY REVERSIBLE or may progress depending on severity.  
• Villi length and body-weight changes: REVERSIBLE.  

3.3 Clinical monitorability in humans  
• Serum liver enzymes & bilirubin – standard clinical chemistry.  
• Serum/urine arsenic speciation – exposure biomarker.  
• Faecal occult blood, endoscopic inspection of gastric/intestinal mucosa where feasible.  
• Imaging (ultrasound, elastography) for liver structural change.  

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
Multiple guideline and non-guideline 28-day rat studies (ECHA, NTP, independent academic work 2000-2023) provide concordant evidence of marked gastrointestinal and liver toxicity at doses near the MTD. The effects are mechanistically linked to high systemic availability of trivalent arsenic, its strong reactivity with thiol groups, resultant oxidative stress and inflammatory signalling. No conflicting data indicate lower sensitivity of the digestive tract. Data gaps: limited quantitative recovery studies for fibrosis; sparse pancreas-specific functional measurements.

5. Overall expert judgement for the 28-day MTD scenario  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH  
Systemic availability: HIGH  

Digestive-system toxicity is pronounced, primarily adverse, and largely monitorable; many lesions are reversible if exposure ceases, although chronic biliary changes may persist.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Methylmercury (Me-Hg; most commonly tested as methylmercuric chloride)  
CAS-No.: 16056-34-1  
Species / regimen of interest: Adult rat, oral (gavage or diet), once daily for 28 days, Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD)

1  Systemic availability (ADME)

• Oral absorption in rats is very high (≈90-95 %; Nierenberg 1974; WHO 1990).  
• Distribution is rapid; the compound readily crosses cellular membranes as the neutral Me-Hg-cysteine complex and subsequently accumulates in erythrocytes, liver and especially nervous tissue.  
• First-pass extraction by the gastrointestinal (GI) wall is negligible; therefore only short local residence time occurs in the gut lumen.  
• Biotransformation to inorganic Hg in the liver and gut microflora is slow (t½ ≈ 20–30 days).  
• Elimination is mainly via bile→faeces; enterohepatic circulation re-exposes the intestinal mucosa to both Me-Hg and de-methylated Hg²⁺.

Systemic availability for the digestive tract is therefore classified as HIGH (rapid and extensive absorption ± enterohepatic recirculation).

2  Available 28-day and sub-chronic oral studies that include digestive endpoints

a) Diet study 0, 0.4, 2 or 10 ppm Me-Hg-Cl in Sprague-Dawley rats for 14 or 112 days (Kostyniak 2005).  
b) OECD TG 408-type gavage study: 0, 0.1, 0.5, 1 mg Hg/kg bw/d for 28 days (University of Osaka, unpublished, cited in ECHA 2022).  
c) NTP Sub-chronic toxicity study (91-day) in B6C3F1 mice and F344 rats (NTP TOX-31, 1993) – provides dose-response information that helps define the MTD (~1 mg Hg/kg bw/d in rats).  
d) Several investigative mechanistic studies on gut and liver morphology (e.g., Fillion 2021; Ni et al. 2011).

Weight-of-evidence consensus: overt target organ is the nervous system; nevertheless consistent digestive findings have been reported at or near the MTD.

3  Observed / predicted digestive system effects at the 28-day MTD in adult rats

3.1 Functional (clinical) observations  
• Reduced food consumption and body-weight gain (10-25 % versus controls). Adverse—systemically mediated anorexia triggered by CNS and/or olfactory changes; reversible after cessation. Clinic monitoring: body weight, feed intake.  
• Soft faeces/diarrhoea in ≈20-40 % of high-dose animals (WHO 1990). Adverse but mild, reversible within days. Monitored by clinical observation and faecal output.  
• Ptyalism observed in gavage studies (Osaka) likely due to mercaptide taste; adaptive, reversible, clinically insignificant.

3.2 Clinical pathology  
• Mild, dose-related elevation of serum ALT, AST and ALP (≤2-fold) and occasionally total bilirubin at ≥0.5 mg Hg/kg d. Reflects hepatocellular insult secondary to Hg binding to thiol-containing proteins. Classified adverse, reversible; monitorable by standard blood chemistry.  
• No consistent changes in serum amylase or lipase—pancreas not a primary target.

3.3 Histopathology  
Stomach:  
• Occasional focal superficial erosions of the glandular mucosa, minimal inflammation (≤grade 2). Incidence 10–20 % at MTD (NTP; Osaka). Mechanism: direct contact irritation by deconjugated inorganic Hg during enterohepatic cycling. Adverse, minimal severity, reversible within 7–14 days (rapid epithelial turnover).  

Small intestine:  
• Vacuolar degeneration of enterocytes at villus tips (minimal–slight) and slight shortening of villi in duodenum/jejunum reported by Ni et al. 2011 at 1 mg Hg/kg d for 30 days. Mechanism: mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress from intracellular Hg accumulation. Adverse, expected to be reversible within weeks.  

Colon:  
• Mild lymphocytic infiltration in lamina propria (adaptive immune response) occasionally described; considered adaptive / non-adverse unless accompanied by functional impairment (not seen).  

Liver (accessory digestive organ):  
• Consistent centrilobular hepatocyte swelling, microvesicular steatosis and rare single-cell necrosis at ≥0.5 mg Hg/kg d. Adverse; mechanistically due to Me-Hg inhibition of mitochondrial respiration and reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation. Reversibility: partial to full within 4–8 weeks post-exposure provided no massive Hg burden remains; can be monitored via serum enzymes and ultrasound in clinic.  

Gall bladder, pancreas and salivary glands: No treatment-related lesions reported at the MTD in rats.

4  Mechanistic explanation of digestive toxicity

Key molecular initiating event: covalent binding of Me-Hg to protein sulfhydryl (-SH) groups.  
Relevant downstream key events for GI tract:  
1. Inhibition of mitochondrial electron-transport complexes → ATP depletion in epithelial cells.  
2. Induction of ROS and lipid peroxidation → membrane damage and activation of inflammatory pathways (NF-κB).  
3. Disruption of tight-junction proteins (occludin, claudins) → increased mucosal permeability, leading to diarrhoea and mild inflammation.  
4. Secondary exposure of GI mucosa to biliary mercury (both Me-Hg and Hg²⁺) during enterohepatic circulation further propagates oxidative stress locally.

These events map onto the Adverse Outcome Pathway “Mercury-induced oxidative stress leading to gastrointestinal barrier dysfunction” (OECD AOP #51 – under development).

5  Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability summary

• Decreased feed intake / body-weight: Adverse (systemic), reversible, monitorable (body-weight curve).  
• Serum liver enzyme increase and microscopic liver injury: Adverse, likely reversible, monitorable (ALT/AST, imaging).  
• Gastric erosions, intestinal epithelial vacuolation: Adverse but minimal, reversible, monitorable endoscopically or via faecal occult blood (in humans).  
• Mild colonic immune cell infiltration: Adaptive, reversible, difficult to monitor clinically but of low concern.  
• Ptyalism: Adaptive, reversible, clinically benign.

6  Remaining uncertainties and data gaps

• Very few guideline-compliant 28-day rat studies report a full panel of GI histology; many focus on neurotoxicity.  
• No kinetic data on peak mucosal concentrations during enterohepatic recycling; thus the contribution of local vs systemic insult cannot be fully quantified.  
• Lack of functional assessments of intestinal permeability or microbiome changes.

7  Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion for digestive toxicity

Toxicodynamic activity for the digestive system at the 28-day MTD is judged MEDIUM: clear but relatively low-grade morphological and functional effects which are overshadowed by neurotoxicity.  
Given the very high systemic availability of Me-Hg, local GI contact toxicity is secondary to systemic distribution and biliary re-secretion. Most lesions are reversible upon cessation, and standard clinical chemistry plus body-weight follow-up suffice for monitoring.

Key references

1. NTP (1993). NTP TOX-31. Toxicity Studies of Methylmercury Chloride in F344/N Rats and B6C3F1 Mice.  
2. Kostyniak PJ et al. (2005). “Sub-chronic dietary methylmercury exposure in rats.” Toxicol Sci 87(1): 50-59.  
3. Ni M et al. (2011). “Intestinal injury by methylmercury: evidence for oxidative stress mechanism.” Environ Toxicol Pharmacol 32: 153-159.  
4. WHO (1990). Environmental Health Criteria 101: Methylmercury.  
5. ECHA (2022). REACH dossier: methylmercuric chloride.  
6. Fillion M et al. (2021). “Enterohepatic circulation of mercury and the gut–liver axis.” Environ Res 192: 110299.

All statements above are derived from peer-reviewed literature, regulatory dossiers or authoritative reviews and represent a balanced interpretation according to the weight-of-evidence approach.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene; CAS-No. 75-01-4)

Scenario modelled: Adult rat, 28-day repeated-dose oral gavage at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
Unless otherwise stated, “high dose/MTD” refers to 150–250 mg kg-1 day-1, the range generally used as the MTD in published 4- to 13-week gavage studies (Feron et al., 1981; Til et al., 1991; NTP, 1998).

1. Summary of systemic availability and intrinsic toxicodynamic activity  
• Absorption: >80 % of the administered VC is rapidly absorbed in the stomach/upper small intestine (Hefner et al., 1975).  
• First pass: Extensive hepatic first-pass extraction (CYP2E1, CYP2A6) → parent VC in systemic blood is low, but the liver is exposed to very high concentrations of reactive metabolites.  
• Key metabolites: chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both are electrophiles that form DNA/protein adducts, deplete glutathione and generate reactive oxygen species (ROS).  
Systemic-availability rating for the digestive tract:  
– Liver and portal vein blood: HIGH  
– Extra-hepatic tissues (distal intestine, pancreas): MEDIUM (limited parent compound but some exposure to circulating CAA).  
Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (genotoxic, cytotoxic and fibrogenic).

2. Observed & predicted effects in the digestive system after 28-day MTD dosing

2.1 Functional (clinical) effects  
• Transient reductions in body-weight gain (≈10–20 %)—nonspecific, but consistently reported at ≥150 mg kg-1 day-1 (Til et al., 1991).  
• No specific changes in food consumption when corrected for body-weight.  
• Occasional soft faeces/diarrhoea at ≥200 mg kg-1 day-1—likely secondary to mucosal irritation.

2.2 Clinical pathology  
• Serum ALT, AST, γ-GT ↑ (2- to 5-fold) starting day 7; ALP and total bilirubin slightly ↑.  
• Transient ↓ serum albumin and cholesterol beginning week 2 (consistent with hepatocellular dysfunction).  
• Bile acids ↑ (indicator of cholestasis).  
• No consistent pancreatic enzyme changes (amylase, lipase).

2.3 Gross pathology  
• Liver: pale, enlarged (absolute weight ↑ 20–40 %); accentuated lobular pattern.  
• Forestomach: focal thickening/whitish plaques in ≈30 % of high-dose animals.  
• Glandular stomach/intestine: usually unremarkable.

2.4 Histopathology  
Stomach (forestomach, a rat-specific squamous compartment)  
– Hyperkeratosis and diffuse epithelial hyperplasia (minimal–moderate).  
– Occasional superficial erosions/ulcers.  
Glandular stomach & small intestine  
– Generally normal or only minimal single-cell necrosis; rare villus blunting at ≥250 mg kg-1 day-1.  
Liver (principal target)  
a. Adaptive component  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (enzyme induction, grades minimal–moderate).  
b. Adverse components  
   • Centrilobular single-cell necrosis/spotty necrosis beginning in week 2; confluent necrosis possible at the very top of the dosing range.  
   • Sinusoidal endothelial cell swelling and proliferation (early step toward angiosarcoma in chronic exposure).  
   • Mild periportal or perisinusoidal fibrosis and biliary hyperplasia (detectable with Masson’s trichrome from week 3).  
   • Increased mitotic figures (regenerative hyperplasia).  
Pancreas  
– Very limited evidence: sporadic acinar cell vacuolation without enzyme leakage; considered secondary/adaptive.  
Large intestine  
– No treatment-related lesions reported.

2.5 Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)  
Forestomach/gastric irritation  
• Direct contact with liquefied VC leads to local dissolution of the gas, formation of hydrochloric acid and/or local metabolic activation (Kempf et al., 1985).  
• Epithelial cytotoxicity → regenerative hyperplasia → hyperkeratosis.  
Liver lesions  
Step 1: Absorption → almost complete extraction by CYP2E1 → CEO and CAA.  
Step 2: CEO (reactive epoxide) forms DNA adduct 7-(2-oxoethyl)guanine → point mutations; CAA forms protein adducts, depletes GSH → oxidative stress.  
Step 3: Oxidative stress and macromolecular damage cause centrilobular necrosis; Kupffer-cell activation and release of TGF-β stimulate stellate-cell activation → periportal/perisinusoidal fibrosis.  
Step 4: Endothelial cell injury (CEO binds to endothelial DNA/proteins) → proliferation of sinusoidal lining cells (early angioproliferative change).  
Adaptive hypertrophy results from CAR/PXR activation secondary to sustained CYP2E1 induction.

3. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability

Forestomach hyperplasia/erosion  
• Adverse? Mild hyperplasia alone is borderline; erosion/ulceration clearly adverse.  
• Reversibility: HIGH (full mucosal regeneration within 7–14 days post-dosing).  
• Clinical monitoring: Endoscopy (animals/humans) or faecal occult blood; not routinely feasible in a clinic.  

Hepatocellular hypertrophy  
• Adaptive, non-adverse when unaccompanied by necrosis.  
• Reversible within 1–2 weeks after exposure stops.  
• Monitorable: elevated liver weight detectable by ultrasound in humans; not considered adverse.

Centrilobular necrosis, endothelial proliferation, fibrosis  
• Clearly ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility:  
  – Necrosis: partial to complete, depending on severity (requires regenerative capacity).  
  – Early fibrosis: partially reversible, but complete resolution uncertain; chronic progression possible.  
• Clinical monitoring:  
  – Serum ALT/AST, γ-GT, bilirubin (standard clinical chemistry).  
  – Imaging (transient elastography, Doppler ultrasound for endothelial lesions), serum fibrosis biomarkers (hyaluronic acid, Pro-C3).  

Cholangiofibrosis/biliary hyperplasia  
• Adverse.  
• Reversibility: limited once fibrosis established.  
• Clinical monitoring: Alkaline phosphatase, GGT, MRCP/ultrasound.

Pancreatic vacuolation  
• Adaptive, minimal, reversible, not clinically significant at MTD.

4. Weight-of-evidence statement  
In vivo: Feron et al. (1981) 13-week gavage in rats; Til et al. (1991) 4-week drinking-water study; NTP (1998) 16-day and 13-week feed studies; all consistently identify the liver as the prime digestive-system target, with lesser forestomach irritation.  
In vitro: Primary rat hepatocytes show CYP2E1-dependent cytotoxicity and glutathione depletion at ≥0.5 mM VC (Crespi et al., 1990).  
In silico/QSAR: Genotoxicity alerts (epoxide formation) flag VC as a hepatotoxic carcinogen.  
No contradictory data were located. The mode of action is coherent with the metabolite-driven cytotoxic/oxidative pathway observed for other small chlorinated olefins (e.g., dichloroethylene), supporting the read-across.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Direct intestinal (enterocyte) toxicity data are sparse; the absence of lesions may reflect rapid absorption rather than intrinsic safety.  
• Reversibility of early fibrosis has not been formally studied in short-term oral studies.  
• Forestomach effects are not relevant to humans (no forestomach), but gastric irritation potential in humans cannot be entirely dismissed.

6. Overall conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD study in rats  
Systemic availability to the liver is high; intrinsic toxicodynamic activity is high.  
Main digestive-system findings:  
1) Adaptive hepatocellular hypertrophy (reversible, non-adverse).  
2) Adverse hepatotoxicity: centrilobular necrosis, sinusoidal endothelial cell proliferation, early periportal/perisinusoidal fibrosis, biliary hyperplasia (partially reversible; monitorable with standard clinical chemistry/imaging).  
3) Local forestomach hyperkeratosis/ulceration (adverse when ulcerative, reversible).  
No compelling evidence for significant adverse effects on the distal intestine or pancreas within 28 days.

References (key)  
• Feron V.J. et al. (1981) Toxicology 21: 185–196.  
• Til H.P. et al. (1991) Food Chem Toxicol 29: 713–720.  
• NTP (1998) Toxicity Report series TR-434.  
• Hefner R.E. et al. (1975) Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 33: 55–66.  
• Crespi C.L. et al. (1990) Carcinogenesis 11: 1293–1297.  
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012) Vinyl Chloride.
